nodes.title: Make the nav a wrapping flex row
nodes.why: The navbar is a one-dimensional row, so Flexbox handles horizontal alignment, vertical centering, spacing, and wrapping without extra wrappers.
nodes.alternative: Use `justify-content: space-between` alone and let the children distribute themselves.
nodes.tradeoff: Explicit flex and gap rules are easier to reason about, while space-between can make the link group drift when content changes.

nodes.title: Keep links as a visible flex list
nodes.why: The list itself needs Flexbox so the links remain a readable row, can wrap when needed, and use gap instead of fragile margins.
nodes.alternative: Center text inside the list or hide links at small widths.
nodes.tradeoff: A visible wrapping list stays CSS-only and accessible, but it needs a breakpoint check for narrow screens.

nodes.title: Push the CTA only in the wrapping variant
nodes.why: This variant keeps the links content-sized, so `margin-left:auto` can push the CTA to the end without fighting a centered flex:1 link group.
nodes.alternative: Let `.links` take the middle space with `flex:1` and `justify-content:center`.
nodes.tradeoff: Auto margin is compact for an end-aligned action, while a flexible link group is better when centered links are the primary requirement.

nodes.title: Reset narrow-width spacing so nothing disappears
nodes.why: At 480px and below, the link group and CTA must remain visible and clickable instead of overflowing or being pushed off-screen.
nodes.alternative: Hide links and replace them with a hamburger menu.
nodes.tradeoff: Keeping links visible avoids JavaScript for this challenge, but it means the layout may take more vertical space.

nodes.title: Preserve keyboard focus styles
nodes.why: Navbar links and the CTA are interactive controls, so keyboard users need a visible focus location while tabbing.
nodes.alternative: Remove outlines for a cleaner visual state.
nodes.tradeoff: Explicit focus styling adds a visible state, but it keeps the navigation usable and interview-ready.

nodes.title: Create the desktop flex row
nodes.why: The desktop navbar needs a single row with vertical centering and predictable spacing between brand, links, and CTA.
nodes.alternative: Use text alignment or inline-block spacing on each child.
nodes.tradeoff: Flexbox gives clearer alignment and less spacing drift than inline or text-only layout.

nodes.title: Let the link group own the center
nodes.why: When centered links are the goal, `.links` should take available space with `flex:1` and center its own children.
nodes.tradeoff: The centered-link approach is visually balanced, but it should not be mixed with auto margin on the CTA.

nodes.title: Stack the nav at the mobile breakpoint
nodes.why: At max-width:480px, stacking creates enough horizontal room for the brand, link group, and CTA without overlap.
nodes.alternative: Keep the row and rely on shrinking or fixed widths.
nodes.tradeoff: Stacking uses more vertical space, but it avoids overflow with real link labels.

nodes.title: Wrap links instead of hiding them
nodes.why: The CSS-only prompt keeps navigation links visible, so the mobile link group should wrap and remain clickable.
nodes.alternative: Hide links behind a JavaScript hamburger menu.
nodes.tradeoff: Wrapping is simpler and accessible for this exercise, while a disclosure menu is a different JavaScript challenge.

nodes.title: Keep focus outlines visible
nodes.why: A strong interview answer preserves keyboard focus on nav links and the CTA instead of removing outlines.
nodes.tradeoff: Focus-visible styling adds an extra state, but it protects keyboard accessibility.
